mnemonics.title: Floyd's Cycle Detection
mnemonics.content: SLOW AND STEADY WINS THE RACE - slow pointer takes 1 step, fast takes 2. If they meet, CYCLE exists. If fast reaches NULL, NO CYCLE.

mnemonics.title: Find Nth From End
mnemonics.content: THE GAP METHOD - Keep TWO pointers n APART. When RIGHT pointer hits NULL, LEFT pointer is at Nth from END.

mnemonics.title: Merge Sorted Lists
mnemonics.content: PICK THE SMALLER - Compare heads, attach smaller to result, move that pointer. When one list ends, attach the rest. DONE.

mnemonics.title: Reverse Linked List
mnemonics.content: THREE POINTER RULE - PREV NULL, CURR HEAD, NEXT SAVE. Reverse CURR's link to PREV. Move all three forward. When CURR NULL, PREV is new HEAD.

mnemonics.title: Palindrome Check
mnemonics.content: MIDDLE REVERSE COMPARE - Find middle with slow/fast. Reverse second half. Compare first half with reversed second. All match? PALINDROME.

mnemonics.title: Clone With Random Pointer
mnemonics.content: HASH MAP TWO PASS - First pass: CREATE clones and MAP old to new. Second pass: SET next and random using MAP. CLONE complete.

mnemonics.title: Rotate Right By K
mnemonics.content: CONNECT AND BREAK - Connect last to first (circle). Move to new head position (n-k). Break the circle. ROTATION complete.

acronyms.expansion: Fast Low Yielding O(1) Detection - cycle detection algorithm
acronyms.usage: Algorithm name breakdown for memorization: F(ast) L(ow) Y(ielding) O(1) D(etection)

acronyms.expansion: Speed variation technique: SLOW moves 1 node, FAST moves 2 nodes
acronyms.usage: Used for middle finding, cycle detection in linked lists

acronyms.expansion: Last In First Out vs First In First Out - stack vs queue approach
acronyms.usage: Understanding auxiliary data structures for list operations

visualTips.visualization: Imagine two runners on a track. Runner B starts n positions ahead. When Runner B reaches finish line, Runner A has run exactly n positions from the end.
Think of a car (fast) and bicycle (slow) on a circular track. The car will eventually lap the bicycle. If it's a straight road (no cycle), the car reaches the end first.
Like merging two sorted card decks - always pick the smaller top card and place in the new merged deck. Continue until one deck is empty.
Undo buttons on a remote - going backward through connections. Each node simply points to its previous node instead of next.
Two trains on different tracks that merge - align them by backing up the longer track, then they travel together to the merge point.

analogies.analogy: Two runners on a circular stadium track. If one runs twice as fast as the other, they WILL meet if the track is circular. They will NOT meet if it's a straight line (no cycle).
In a relay race, the coach watches from the midpoint. When the last runner passes the coach, the first runner has just started their second lap - that's the middle.
Merging two sorted book piles on a shelf - always pick the smaller ISBN book from the top of either pile and place it on the new shelf.
Reversing a train direction on a track - each carriage (node) simply turns to face the opposite direction, and the engine becomes the last carriage.
Building a copy of a city with landmark directions - first build all buildings (nodes), then use a map to know which random directions to connect.
A rotating car turntable - connecting the last position to the first creates a circle, moving the break point determines the new starting position.
Two roads merging onto a highway - align them at the merge point by starting the longer road further back, then both travel to the intersection together.
